explanation.text: Cartographers could only draw the southern regions vaguely and labeled them Unknown Southern Land because they did not know what was there. This uncertainty emphasizes how limited geographic knowledge was when Great Britain chose Cook to explore.

Cook used his reading to study navigation and then took on increasing responsibility as a navigator aboard merchant ships. That advancement supports the claim that his literacy and ambition distinguished him from older sailors.

The article explains that even a small map error could send a ship hundreds of miles off course and delay its search for food or water. Cartographers were therefore valued because their accurate information helped ships navigate safely.

Cook’s calculations were so accurate that his reputation grew, and he successfully charted the Canadian border. His later selection to lead the South Seas expedition further demonstrates his effectiveness at drawing reliable charts.

Before electronic navigation, sailors used instruments to determine a ship’s position relative to the sun and stars. This explains why astronomy provided essential reference points for traveling accurately at sea.

Cook successfully completed the Venus expedition, explored New Zealand and eastern Australia, and drew accurate maps of the region. These achievements develop the article’s central idea by showing his important contributions to exploration.

The article moves in time from Cook’s childhood and apprenticeship through his naval advancement and major expeditions. This chronological sequence shows how his education, discipline, and mapping skills led to increasingly significant accomplishments.

The author mentions playing basketball and volleyball herself and describes her son’s and daughter’s sports activities. These family experiences show her personal connection to youth sports before she discusses their risks.

The article acknowledges that combat and sports are very different situations, but says repeated head injuries in both can have similar consequences. That shared outcome explains why evidence from injured veterans has informed the study of athletes with concussions.

To walk off an injury means to keep moving and treat it as a minor problem that will quickly pass. The author says players can no longer do this because even an ordinary-looking bump may cause serious brain damage.

Minnesota has enacted education requirements about head injuries and requires medical clearance before a student returns to play. These concrete reforms support the central idea that progress is being made to make youth sports safer.

The example involves more than two thousand retired professional football players, not children or school teams. Their lawsuit over concussion dangers supports the inference that head injuries affect professional as well as student athletes.

A study documented more than one hundred head impacts for an average second-grade player, and Pop Warner responded by limiting contact during practice. This example directly supports the claim because it shows an organization using existing knowledge to let children play more safely.

The article reports hundreds of thousands of sports-related head injuries and describes young football players experiencing impacts comparable to car accidents. This evidence supports the claim that participation in youth sports can expose children to real risks.

Terry considers his familiar choices of television, snacks, or bed, yet he is already moving toward the garage. His unfinished thought and movement toward the kit car show that he wants to do something different from those usual activities.

The passage inventories Terry’s complete set of sockets, wrenches, gauges, and specialized tools, all kept in excellent condition. These details show that he already has the equipment needed to begin working on the kit car.

Terry carefully wipes every tool before returning it and checks that every item is in its proper drawer. This consistent care reveals pride in his possessions rather than unfamiliarity or fear that they are missing.

Terry bought the clock as a child in an unsuccessful attempt to get his mother’s attention, which reveals something about their relationship and his emotional history. His later shock at the clock’s time also shows how completely he becomes absorbed in the car, so the clock deepens the reader’s understanding of him.

Terry organizes parts for hours without noticing his hunger or the passing time, then is stunned to see that it is three in the morning. This shows that time seems to stop because he is deeply absorbed in the task.

Before finding the instructions, Terry despairs of ever understanding the many unfamiliar parts. His excited claim that even a monkey could follow the detailed guide shows his change from discouragement to confidence and enthusiasm.

The garage contains the unfinished kit car, Terry’s complete tool set, and the detailed instructions hidden among the parts. This shows how the setting makes the project seem possible and inspires him to organize the pieces and begin work.

The sentences identify chefs, cafeteria workers, administrators, politicians, and others across the country who are determined to change school food. This broad coalition supports the central idea that many people are seeking to improve the nutritional value of school lunches.

Poorly trained workers with inadequate equipment produce billions of inexpensive lunches, often repeating breaded meat, fries, and overcooked vegetables. In this context, churn out suggests mass production with little careful planning rather than thoughtful preparation.

Rising childhood obesity and diabetes establish the serious health problem that the proposed lunch reforms are meant to address. This information belongs in a summary because it explains why providing students with healthier meal options is important.

The article says that home cooking is becoming a forgotten art while children are surrounded by fast-food advertising. This indicates that many families are not preparing meals at home, creating an obstacle to better nutrition outside school.

In Santa Fe, volunteers first taught a cooking class, then struggled with kitchens designed only to reheat food, and finally obtained basic tools through a grant. This sequence uses one district to demonstrate that improving school food requires gradual steps through practical obstacles.

The school kitchens could reheat prepared food but could not prepare fresh ingredients, and the cooks needed grant-funded knives just to peel and chop produce. These details support the inference that the kitchens were not equipped to serve fresh foods.

Schools can earn as much as seventy-five thousand dollars each year from soda and candy machines. Because students spend less on healthier replacements, changing the food means giving up income from popular unhealthy snacks.
